does: This paper investigates how reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) can be trained efficiently from minimal interactions in open-ended environments and whether the conversational abilities learned during this process transfer implicitly to specialized downstream tasks like mathematical reasoning. The authors study the generalization properties of RLHF trained from scratch with limited data and compute, evaluating transfer across domains. The core contribution is demonstrating that conversational abilities acquired through RLHF on general tasks can provide implicit gains on reasoning-oriented tasks, potentially reducing the need for extensive domain-specific RL training and the associated computational overhead.
connects: This work bridges recent developments in post-training paradigms for large language models, positioning itself between RLHF and RLVR approaches. It responds to the tension in recent scaling literature where RLVR with verifiable rewards dominates reasoning tasks but requires substantial compute, while RLHF remains underexplored for generalization. The paper builds on prior work investigating transfer learning in language models and extends research into how preference-based learning from human feedback can improve model behavior across diverse settings. It connects to broader questions about alignment and capability transfer in foundation models, addressing a practical gap between these two RL paradigms.
enables: This research opens pathways toward more computationally efficient post-training of language models by identifying which capabilities learned through general RLHF transfer to specialized domains without additional task-specific RL. If successful, it could enable practitioners with limited compute budgets to benefit from RL post-training through strategic transfer rather than domain-specific optimization. The work positions future investigation into which task distributions best support transfer learning in RL-based post-training and could inform more efficient strategies for scaling alignment methods. It may also motivate hybrid approaches combining general RLHF with minimal domain-specific verification, reducing the computational barrier to adopting reinforcement learning for model improvement.
